perturbation.original_text: As used herein, "Business Day" means any day other than a Saturday, Sunday or other day on which banking institutions in New York, New York are authorized or required by law or executive order to remain closed.
perturbation.changed_text: As used herein, "Business Day" means any day other than a Saturday or Sunday, regardless of whether banking institutions in New York, New York are authorized or required by law or executive order to remain closed.
perturbation.explanation: The original definition of 'Business Day' aligns with standard legal and financial practice, considering bank holidays. The modified definition excludes bank holidays, which introduces a contradiction. For instance, if the Remarketing Settlement Date falls on a federal holiday when banks are closed in New York, the modified definition would still consider it a 'Business Day,' which could create confusion and potential breach of contract.
perturbation.law_explanation: New York General Construction Law § 24 defines holidays and states that when a holiday falls on a Sunday, the following Monday is considered a public holiday. By stating that a 'Business Day' excludes only Saturdays and Sundays regardless of bank closures mandated by law or executive order, the modified text directly contradicts the state law recognizing certain Mondays as legal holidays, creating uncertainty in contractual obligations.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: NYS Open Legislation | NYSenate.gov Sorry, you need to enable JavaScript to visit this website. Skip to main content The requested entry could not be found. Try searching OpenLegislation Browse all Laws of New York
perturbation.justification: The modified text redefines 'Business Day' in a way that contradicts standard legal and financial practices, specifically by disregarding bank holidays. The original text includes days when banking institutions in New York are authorized or required to remain closed, while the changed text excludes only Saturdays and Sundays. The Law Explanation points out that New York General Construction Law § 24 recognizes certain Mondays as legal holidays when a holiday falls on a Sunday. By disregarding these holidays, the modified definition creates a situation where a day legally recognized as a holiday could still be considered a 'Business Day' under the contract, creating uncertainty. While Scraped Law Snippet 1 is unavailable, this deviation constitutes a contradiction, falling under the 'Misaligned Terminology - Legal Contradiction' definition, because the contractual language deviates from legally recognized definitions, potentially making the contract unenforceable.

perturbation.original_text: The Company and each Significant Subsidiary has good and marketable title in fee simple to all material real property and good and marketable title to all material personal property owned by it, in each case free and clear of all liens, encumbrances and defects that materially interfere with the use made and proposed to be made of such property by the Company or any Significant Subsidiary, except such as are described in the Disclosure Package or such as would not have a material adverse effect on the business, financial position, equity, reserves, surplus or results of operations of the Company and its subsidiaries, considered as a whole ("Material Adverse Effect"), and any material real property and material buildings held under lease by the Company or any of its subsidiaries are held under valid, subsisting and enforceable leases with such exceptions that do not materially interfere with the use made and currently proposed to be made of such property and buildings by the Company or any Significant Subsidiary.
perturbation.changed_text: The Company and each Significant Subsidiary has good and marketable title to all real property and good title to all personal property owned by it, in each case free and clear of all encumbrances, except those described in the Disclosure Package. Any real property and buildings held under lease by the Company or any of its subsidiaries are held under valid leases.
perturbation.explanation: The original clause requires 'good and marketable title in fee simple' for real property, which is a specific legal standard. The modified version removes 'in fee simple' and 'marketable' which are vital components of a valid real estate title. It also omits the phrase 'that materially interfere with the use made and proposed to be made of such property', weakening the protection against encumbrances. The standard for personal property is lowered to 'good title' instead of 'good and marketable title'.
perturbation.contradicted_law: New York Real Property Law Article 12
perturbation.law_explanation: New York Real Property Law Article 12, specifically Sections 290-294, addresses the recording of conveyances and mortgages, which is central to establishing marketable title. Marketable title generally means a title free from reasonable doubt, and an encumbrance is a claim or lien on property that diminishes its value or restricts its use. The modified text weakens the requirement for a clear and marketable title by omitting the phrase 'in fee simple', which signifies absolute ownership, and 'materially interfere with the use' opening up the door to disputes over encumbrances that, while existing, might not have been intended to be covered. The altered language contradicts the protections afforded by the state's real property laws.

perturbation.justification: The modified text contradicts established real property law by weakening the standards for property titles. The original contract specifies 'good and marketable title in fee simple' for real property, which is a legally recognized standard signifying complete ownership rights. The changed text removes 'in fee simple' and 'marketable,' reducing the title standard. It also omits the clause regarding encumbrances materially interfering with the use of the property, further weakening protections. Scraped Law Snippet 1, referencing New York Real Property Law Article 12, indicates the importance of registering title to real property which is at odds with the reduced level of protection that the modified clause provides.

perturbation.original_text: The Company agrees that, unless it obtains the prior consent of the Remarketing Agents, and each Remarketing Agent represents and agrees that, unless it obtains the prior consent of the Company, it has not made and will not make any offer relating to the Remarketed Securities that would constitute an Issuer Free Writing Prospectus (other than, for the avoidance of doubt, any Bloomberg L.P. or other electronic communication regarding any preliminary term sheets or comparable bond prices and the Final Term Sheet filed pursuant to Section 3(b) hereto).
perturbation.changed_text: The Company agrees that it will not make any offer relating to the Remarketed Securities. Each Remarketing Agent represents and agrees that it will not make any offer relating to the Remarketed Securities.
perturbation.explanation: The original text allows for some flexibility and exceptions regarding offers, specifying that no offers should constitute an 'Issuer Free Writing Prospectus' without consent, and explicitly excludes preliminary term sheets and similar communications. The modified text implements a blanket prohibition on 'any offer,' removing any flexibility or permitted communications.
perturbation.law_explanation: 17 CFR § 230.433 defines and regulates the use of free writing prospectuses. The rule allows for certain written communications (free writing prospectuses) to be made after the registration statement has been filed, provided certain conditions are met. By imposing a blanket ban on 'any offer,' the modified contract text contradicts Rule 433, which explicitly permits the use of Issuer Free Writing Prospectuses under specific conditions, thus making it impossible for the Company or Remarketing Agents to avail of the provisions of Rule 433 even when they otherwise could have complied with it.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 17 CFR § 230.433 - Conditions to permissible post-filing free writing prospectuses. | Electronic Code of Federal Regulations (e-CFR) | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you 17 CFR § 230.433 - Conditions to permissible post-filing free writing prospectuses. CFR Table of Popular Names prev | next § 230.433 Conditions to permissible post-filing free writing prospectuses. (a) Scope of section. This section applies to any free writing prospectus with respect to securities of any issuer (except as set forth in Rule 164 ( § 230.164 )) that are the subject of a registration statement that has been filed under the Act . Such a free writing prospectus that satisfies the conditions of this section may include information the substance of which is not included in the registration statement. Such a free writing prospectus that satisfies the conditions of this section will be a prospectus permitted under section 10(b) of the Act for purposes of sections 2(a)(10), 5(b)(1), and 5(b)(2) of the Act and will, for purposes of considering it a prospectus , be deemed to be public, without regard to its method of use or distribution , because it is related to the public offering of securities that are the subject of a filed registration statement. (b) Permitted use of free writing prospectus. Subject to the conditions of this paragraph (b) and satisfaction of the conditions set forth in paragraphs (c) through (g) of this section, a free writing prospectus may be used under this section and Rule 164 in connection with a registered offering of securities: (1) Eligibility and prospectus conditions for seasoned issuers, well-known seasoned issuers, and offerings of registered non-variable annuity securities. Subject to the provisions of Rule 164(e), (f), and (g), the issuer or any other offering participant may use a free writing prospectus in the following offerings after a registration statement relating to the offering has been filed that includes a pro
perturbation.justification: The modified contract text contradicts 17 CFR § 230.433, which outlines the conditions for permissible post-filing free writing prospectuses. The original text allows for certain offers related to Remarketed Securities, with exceptions for specific communications. The changed text imposes a blanket ban on 'any offer,' removing the flexibility permitted under Rule 433. The scraped law snippet 2 states that 'Subject to the conditions of this paragraph (b) and satisfaction of the conditions set forth in paragraphs (c) through (g) of this section, a free writing prospectus may be used under this section and Rule 164 in connection with a registered offering of securities'. By removing the possibility of using free writing prospectuses, the modified contract directly contradicts the conditions provided in the law. This contradiction is significant because it removes a legally permissible option for communication related to securities offerings, which constitutes misaligned terminology and is a legal contradiction.
